instruction: Summarize the main findings of the study in one sentence.
input: Journal of Human Kinetics volume 86/2023, 97–105 DOI: 10.5114/jhk/159451 Differences in Velocities of Crucial Body Segments while Executing Roundhouse Kicks for Both Sides ...
output: The study found higher correlations between the maximal velocity of certain body segments and the toe marker for left kicks, despite no significant difference in maximal velocity between sides.

instruction: State the main limitation of the study as mentioned in the text.
input: Journal of Human Kinetics volume 86/2023, 97–105 DOI: 10.5114/jhk/159451 ...
output: The study did not reveal significant differences between maximal velocity, limiting its use as a performance benchmark.

instruction: Summarize the main finding about roundhouse kick velocity in the provided text.
output: The study reports maximum roundhouse kick velocities of 13.24 m/s (Muay Thai), 13.66 m/s (Karate), 17.35 m/s (Kickboxing), and ~16 m/s (Taekwondo), indicating that velocity is highest in kickboxing.

instruction: What is the maximum velocity of a roundhouse kick for karate according to the text?
Identify the biomechanical principle linking arm swing width to kick power in the text.
output: According to Newton’s second law of rotational motion, a wider arm swing generates greater torque, increasing angular momentum and kick power.

instruction: Summarize the main findings of the study in one sentence.
input: lar momentum will be greater accordingly. In that manner, the power generated by the lower limbs during the execution of the kick will be higher. This premise facilitates the assumption that to fully understand roundhouse kick kinematics, a full body analysis of an athlete is required. The aim of this study was to determine how the velocity of chosen body segments of taekwon-do athletes affected the maximal velocity of the foot during the execution of a roundhouse kick and to determine the similarities and differences between both kicking lower extremities. Methods Participants There were 13 participants in this study, all of whom were elite taekwon-do ITF (International Taekwon-do Federation) athletes (aged: 22.6 ± 6.28 years; body mass: 73.76 ± 10.16 kg; body height: 177.69 ± 6.78 cm), with at least 4 years of fighting experience. Participants declared that their preferred kicking leg was the right one. Measures For the purpose of this study, a stereo photogrammetric method of motion capture was used. The study was conducted in a Human Motion Lab (HML) equipped with 10 infrared cameras NIR Vicon MX-T40 with a resolution of 4 megapixels and a 10-bit grey scale. This system is capable of capturing 370 frames per second. In order to analyse the motion of all key segments of the body, ten markers were used. Two markers were placed on the side of the proximal phalanx great toe (rtoe, ltoe), on the side of the knee (rkne, lkne – r stands for right side, l for the left side), the anterior superior iliac spine (rasi, lasi), the sternum (strn), the acromial end of clavicle (rclo, lclo), on the side of the elbows (relb, lelb), and both hands (rhno, lhno). Spatial-temporal data of marker displacement in time function for X,Y, Z plane were captured and stored in c3d format data. With the use of algorithms written in python with the use of pyomeca library (Martinez et al., 2020), the resultant velocity was computed for all markers and the maximal velocity for each motion was determined. Design and Procedures Participants executed a taekwon-do version of the roundhouse kick. It is a circular motion kick performed from a side stance. Upon raising the kicking leg, there is rotation on the supporting leg, bending in the knee and the hip joint in the kicking leg, together with torso rotation toward a target with the corresponding side of the body and finally knee extension to reach the target with the foot. All strikes were performed on a table tennis ball hanging on a string from the ceiling. The target was non-resistant and athletes performed 3 strikes for each side of the body. Overall, 78 kicks were captured. The marker placement and visualization of the movement are presented in Figure 1. Participants were told to measure t
output: The study found that the velocity of body segments affects the maximal foot velocity in a roundhouse kick, with 13 elite taekwon‑do athletes showing higher lower‑limb momentum and power, and that full‑body analysis is needed to understand the kick kinematics.

instruction: Summarize the main result regarding toe marker velocities.
output: The left toe marker recorded a higher maximal velocity (13.19 m/s) compared to the right toe marker (12.94 m/s).

instruction: Extract the mean velocity values for the left and right toe markers.
output: The mean velocities were 13.19 m/s for the left toe marker and 12.94 m/s for the right toe marker.

instruction: Describe the statistical method used to compare velocities across markers.
output: Velocities were analyzed using Pearson correlation coefficients and univariate ANOVA with a significance threshold of p < 0.05.

instruction: What is the mean maximal velocity of the toe marker?
Calculate the standard deviation of toe marker velocities.
Which marker pair has the highest Spearman correlation?
output: toe and hand (0.782)

instruction: Extract the value of the maximal velocity of the toe marker.
Summarize the main finding regarding correlation between body mass and clavicle.
output: Body mass is significantly correlated with the clavicle (r = 0.694).

instruction: Identify which pair of markers had the highest correlation on the kicking side.
output: The highest correlation was between the hand-toe and hand-knee pairing (r = 0.782).

instruction: Extract the velocity values for the distal markers in the studies by Estevan et al., Gavagan et al., Kim et al., and Aandahl et al.
input: h as  pads or shields, they reached velocities of 12.89 m/s in the study conducted by Estevan et al. (2013), while in Gavagan et al.’s (2017) study, it was 14.66 m/s and in Kim et al.’s (2011) study, it was 14.7 m/s, whereas in the study by Aandahl et al. (2018), athletes reached values up to 17.35 m/s.

instruction: Provide the correlation coefficient between the toe marker and knee marker for the roundhouse kick.
Summarize the conclusion regarding the importance of upper limb swing and trunk rotation in the roundhouse kick.
input: It could be concluded that the initial upper limb swing followed by trunk rotation are key elements in the execution of the roundhouse kick.
output: The initial upper limb swing followed by trunk rotation are key elements in the execution of the roundhouse kick.

instruction: Summarize the main findings of the biomechanics study in one sentence.
output: The study found no lateralization effect in roundhouse kicks beyond central body parts, linking maximal velocities across core, limbs, and shoulder girdle.

instruction: Extract the key recommendation from the conclusions.
input: The present study indicates that during the execution of the roundhouse kick by taekwon-do ITF athletes, there is no lateralization effect of the kicking sides besides the central body parts.
output: Recommend performing full-body analysis and analyzing kicking force for better understanding of lateralization.

instruction: Generate a research question based on the text.
input: The data with no lateralization effect besides the central body parts could be considered from the perspective of the concept that complex motor tasks could be changed at the sensory-motor level during its execution, depending on the needs and circumstances.
output: How does the sensory-motor adaptation of roundhouse kicks vary with target type and body segment, influencing lateralization?
